record.title: Regina Kohl | Interview 1 | July 2, 2014

point.title: Meeting Regina Kohl, her family and growing up in Philadelphia
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Regina school years and early years working graveyard shifts as a Riveter
point.keywords: dreams;graveyard shifts;growing up;high school;nursing school;Philadelphia Navy Yard;public transportation;riveter;school;women worker
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Working in the Navy Yard as a Riveter while attending school/ Regina talks about her social life in those years
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Dating while working in the shipyard/ Deciding to become a nurse
point.keywords: 1940s;dating;graveyard shifts;Navy Yard;nursing school;Philadelphia Navy Yard;shipyard;war time
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Voluntiring to become a air warden and concerns with the war/ Regina talks abotu Pearl Harbor
point.keywords: air warden;blackouts;graveyard shifts;independence;Pearl Harbor;Philadelphia;volunteers;war concerns;war time
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Regina talks about wanting to enlist in the US Army or the WAVES/ Working int he Navy Yard
point.keywords: air raid;enlist;family;Navy yard;nursing school;parents;Philadelphia;soldiers;US Army;WAVES;wing flaps
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Regina explain how she met her husband
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Regina talks about the end of the war and her community growing up
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Regina explains why she did not pursue nursing and talks about the financial situation during war
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Regina talks about her mother and her sister/ work clothing and what she learned from her mother
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Her grandparents, family life and unity
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Regina talks women's fashion/ Homosexuality, divesity and entertainment during the war
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Regina talks marriege and life as a grandmother
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Her husband returning to college after the war/ Living in Buchanan, West Virginia in the 1950s
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Becoming an elementary teacher in Philadelphia/ Working in beauty pageants
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

point.title: Regina volunteering in Israel/ End of Interview
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

record.transcript:
DUNHAM: It's July 2, 2014, and this is tape one with Regina Kohl, and we're here in her lovely place here right at the ocean in Atlantic City. Thank you so much for having us today. We're here for the Rosie the Riveter Oral History Project. We usually start at the beginning, so we ask when and where you were born.
KOHL: I was born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, March 23, 1924.
DUNHAM: So you just celebrated your ninetieth birthday this year.
KOHL: Yes.
DUNHAM: Congratulations. Wanted to ask a little family background. I think you showed us some photos earlier of your mother and grandmother. So did you know grandparents on both sides?
KOHL: I knew both my grandparents. My father's parents lived in California, so we didn't know them well, but we did know them. My mother's parents lived in Philadelphia, and we were always there. We were very close. It was wonderful.
DUNHAM: And you grew up in Philadelphia, as well, so--
KOHL: I grew up in Philadelphia. Born and raised in Philadelphia. Went to school, went to college.
DUNHAM: And do you know the ethnic background on your mother's and father's side? Where they came from before they came to the US?
KOHL: My grandparents came from Austria. My father's parents came from Russia. But when they came to the United States they became Americans.
DUNHAM: And what was life like for your mother and father when you were growing up?
KOHL: It was great. It was great.
DUNHAM: Did your father work in a particular field?
KOHL: My father worked at the post office. He worked for the post office in Philadelphia. And my mother didn't work.
DUNHAM: And how many siblings did you have?
KOHL: I had a brother. My brother died last year.
DUNHAM: Oh, I'm sorry. And was older or younger?
KOHL: No, he was thirteen months younger than me. We just had a great family life.
DUNHAM: What did your father do at the post office?
KOHL: He was a clerk.
DUNHAM: In Philadelphia?
KOHL: Oh, in Philadelphia.
DUNHAM: What was Philadelphia like when you were growing up?
KOHL: Well, it certainly was different than it is today, that's for sure. Going to public school. Walked everywhere because we didn't have--my father had a car, but we walked. Everybody walked everywhere, and we walked to school. There are a lot of stories about family. There was gas rationing, so my father drove to work, a post office in Center City.
DUNHAM: Feel free to share, please.
KOHL: I believe there were food rationing stamps. My mother was a good manager so we always had food on the table. Well, I guess the biggest memory I have, which is just wonderful, was my grandmother every Sunday--my cousins and I went to Sunday school--and after Sunday school we went to my grandparents' home for lunch. And my grandmother was a German cook. So the big meal was at lunch. So she cooked. But then about three o'clock in the afternoon my parents would come to my grandparents' home, my aunt and uncle. The family would all come to my grandparents' home on Sunday afternoon between three and four o'clock, and then my grandmother had a big round, I guess a walnut table. Big round table, and we all fit around the table. And we had supper. Everybody had supper together, which was big pieces of fresh rye bread and sweet butter, pots of coffee, and she would open canned salmon and vegetables. And we'd all sit around and eat together. All the cousins, aunts and uncles. It was just great. Everybody knew everyone. It was just wonderful time.
DUNHAM: Did you have cousins that were similar ages to you and your brother?
KOHL: My cousins were a little bit older than me, but we were all together. The numbers didn't seem to count. It was just that we were together all the time. And it was a ritual. It was every Sunday. No one ever made any other plans to do anything except to go to my grandmother and grandfather's and see everyone and have dinner together.
DUNHAM: That's great. What religion did you grow up with?
KOHL: Jewish. I'm Jewish. It was just a great time. And then, of course, the holidays we were always together. We had a great family unit. And I still see a lot of my cousins that are still living. I have a cousin in Florida; she's ninety-four. She was ninety-four in March. I talk to her on the phone. We talk to each other. And my other cousins and siblings.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Well, what was school like for you growing up?
KOHL: School was school. Reading, writing, and arithmetic. It was fun. I look at the kids today with their backpacks, my grandchildren, my great-grandchildren. And we had school bags. We carried our books in school bags. Like I said, everyone walked to school. It's somewhat different today. I guess they walk. From the neighborhood where I grew up, I guess the children still walk to school. I just don't know. No school buses.
DUNHAM: Do you remember when you were a young girl in school? Did you have any particular dreams or ideas of what you might be when you grew up?
KOHL: I dreamed of going to nursing school. And I went to nursing school. I graduated high school in 1942, and I worked the night shift, 4:00 to 12:00, at the Philadelphia Navy Yard, because I went to school during the day. I'd get home around 1:30 in the morning, and my mother would have a pot of homemade soup on the stove. I'd heat the soup, eat the soup, take a shower, go to bed, get up by eight o'clock, go to class, go to work and come home at 1:30 in the morning.
DUNHAM: I want to hear a lot more about your work at the Philadelphia Naval Yard and everything, but may we back up a minute? Your father worked at the post office, so I assume that job was secure all through the Depression? Or did the Depression impact your family or other family members significantly?
KOHL: I really don't think so.
DUNHAM: Everyone was around and still coming to the Sunday supper all through those years? Yes.
KOHL: Oh, that was ongoing. And, of course, I traveled public transportation everywhere I went. And from the Navy Yard I had to take the subway to--Broad and Erie Avenue is where streetcar to my mother's house, and then at one o'clock in the morning or 1:30 I was walking two blocks to go home by myself.
DUNHAM: How did you find out about the job at the Philadelphia Navy Yard?
KOHL: A friend of mine told me about it.
DUNHAM: Was she or he already working there?
KOHL: Yes. She was working there, and she knew someone else that was work--she said, "Apply for the job. Go and see." And I did.
DUNHAM: Had you done other jobs growing up?
KOHL: Yes. Babysitting. Working in a lingerie shop, during Christmas, holidays.
DUNHAM: What was it like at the Philadelphia Navy Yard? What job did you apply for, and what were you doing there?
KOHL: Well, I just applied to work there, and they taught me how to be a riveter, use a band saw, because we had to cut the Plexiglas for the-- I know I had to use the band saw for Plexiglas for the windshields, and wear goggles.
KOHL: Navy airplanes.
KOHL: I can't remember exactly, but I remember doing it. I did learn how to be a riveter. As a riveter I worked on wing flaps. I had a wonderful girl who backed me up with a bar as I put rivets with a gun.
DUNHAM: Was there a training period in advance, or did you learn on the job?
KOHL: Riveting was a two-person job. I learned on the job. On the job. It didn't take much to learn to do it. Just that you had to have someone backing you up. Two people working together simultaneously, because otherwise it wouldn't work. I loved the work and the excitement of helping the war effort.
DUNHAM: What was the makeup of the rest of your crew gender-wise, ethnicity-wise?
KOHL: We were all patriotic helping our own servicemen and to end the war. Mixed. All ages.
DUNHAM: And mostly from the Philadelphia area?
KOHL: All through Philadelphia. Most of them from south Philadelphia. Some people took the subway, went so far from south Philadelphia where the Navy Yard was. It's not there anymore. By the time I got up to the northeast, or north Philadelphia where I lived and took the bus or the streetcar to my mother's house, I was alone.
DUNHAM: You were near the end of the line?
KOHL: No, but no one from the Navy Yard went as far as I did on the streetcar. So then I took the streetcar by myself.
DUNHAM: How did you find the work? Was it fun?
KOHL: Of course. It was an adventure. I didn't know any better. I loved it. I loved the money. Everyone was friendly and our goal was Uncle Sam needed us!
DUNHAM: Do you remember what you got paid?
KOHL: No, I don't. I was trying to think about that, but I don't.
DUNHAM: A lot more than the other jobs you'd probably--
KOHL: Well, it was a lot more than a civilian job. Besides, that gave me time to go to school, work out my schedule, which worked out well.
DUNHAM: And then you had to study and do homework, too?
KOHL: Oh, sure.
DUNHAM: It sounds like a pretty demanding schedule. Did you have time for fun?
KOHL: Sure. I went dancing at the USO. Stage Door Canteen I was a hostess.
DUNHAM: What was that like? Tell us about that.
KOHL: It was great fun. The soldiers and sailors were so adorable. They were so young, much fun. Didn't take any of them home, but had a great time dancing. I loved it. Soldiers, Navy, young fellows away from their families, appreciated the hospitality.
DUNHAM: What was the dating life like there at the Navy Yard or the dances?
KOHL: Just company, not much dating, we worked three shifts. I worked from 4:00 to 12:00. It took me an hour and a half to get home. My father was a very quiet man. The one thing he told me when I graduated high school and I went to go to work, he said, "Don't date anyone you work with. That would be a bad idea." And I didn't. But I had other friends. We all went out together in groups and all, from school, and then, of course, I went to the USO and danced, the Stage Door Canteen, a hostess. I loved it.
DUNHAM: Is the Stage Door Canteen, is that where the USO dances were or that's another place for dances?
KOHL: That was the Stage Door Canteen. One thing in Philadelphia, everything was for the soldier, the serviceman. They could ride the streetcars for free. They could go into the movies for free. They had every place, like the Jewish Y, the YMCA, they always had a setout for the servicemen, soldiers, sailors, marines, whichever. Whoever walked in, they were fed. There was never a question that the serviceman didn't come first. All branches of the service, anywhere in Philadelphia. Of course, I think it should be like that today.
DUNHAM: Aside from the Stage Door Canteen and USO, YMHA, Reyburn Plaza. What's YMHA stand for?
KOHL: The Young People's Hebrew Society. It was the Jewish Y. Then there was the other Y, YMCA, the Christian Y. I did them all. I found time for everything in between everything else I had to do.
DUNHAM: And is this all hostesses for dances or other events? What kind of other activities?
KOHL: No, it was mostly to go and dance and talk to the sailors, make them feel at home, or the soldiers, Make them feel at home because they were so far from home, and they were so young, like me.
DUNHAM: And were there live bands at the dances or recorded music or some of both?
KOHL: Some of both. Depended.
DUNHAM: What kinds of music were you enjoying?
KOHL: Wasn't it rock and roll? No, it was just good music. Big band music Harry James, Tommy Dorsey.
DUNHAM: And you said in your bio that you're a jitterbug fan.
KOHL: I was a good jitterbug.
DUNHAM: When did you learn to jitterbug?
KOHL: Oh, I learned to jitterbug when I was like fourteen years old, twelve years old. We danced. The school dances.
DUNHAM: School dances, yeah.
KOHL: Along the street. Danced to school. Great time.
DUNHAM: Tell me more about life at Philadelphia Navy Yard. You worked a swing shift. What was an average day? You described working with a partner. But were you doing the same kind of thing throughout the day? What kind of breaks did you have or what did you eat?
KOHL: Well, we had a lunch. I guess you'd call it dinner break. And if you needed a break you could take time to go to the bathroom, whatever you needed to do. It was just a job, and they treated you well. I guess because it was the war everybody--I shouldn't say because of the war. But everybody was working together. Everything was togetherness with everything. Everybody was nice. It was a mixture of people. It wasn't any particular--
DUNHAM: You mention that your father--[phone ringing] oh, do you want to--
KOHL: That's mine. Just a minute.
DUNHAM: Sure, just pause. No problem. Go ahead.
DUNHAM: We were just talking about the shipyard. Yeah. I was going to ask, because your father had specifically cautioned you about not dating in the workplace. Did you have friends or other folks that you observed who did date, and any challenges they may have had or successes or what have you?
KOHL: Oh, I don't know. I really don't know. I honestly don't know. All I know is the guys, they would take you out. Of course, they couldn't buy me a drink. But, "Let's go out." First of all, I was working the 4:00 to 12:00 shift. And I'll tell you, by the time I got off and went home, I wasn't about to go out anywhere at 12:30 at night. And I had to get up in the morning and go to school. I couldn't do that.
DUNHAM: You had limited time. But so all the hostessing events were on the weekends then?
KOHL: Yes.
DUNHAM: What was nursing school like? Now, let me ask first, since that had been your dream to do that, had you known nurses in your family or elsewhere, or what had inspired that?
KOHL: No, it was just something I wanted to do. It was something on my mind. My grandmother, she broke a hip, and I went to take care of her. And it was just the way it was.
DUNHAM: How did you find nursing school? Was it a piece of cake or was it challenging?
KOHL: It was challenging. I worked it out. Yeah, it was challenging but it was good.
DUNHAM: Do you mind if I ask what was challenging about it?
KOHL: Well, you had to take care of people and do things that I wasn't used to. But you learn. You get past changing the bed with someone in it and things like that.
DUNHAM: As I said, when I cared for my grandmother, [my wife] Candace and I developed a newfound respect for all nurses and caregivers because we--
KOHL: You see what they have to do.
DUNHAM: Yeah, we did it firsthand. It was a challenge for sure. How long were you in nursing school and/or working at the Navy Yard then?
KOHL: Let's say, two--
DUNHAM: You started the fall of '42, after graduating in June of '42? Is that right?
KOHL: I guess it was '42, '43. And then in '44, 1944--I think I was two years at the Navy Yard. Then I went to Proctor Electric when I heard they were making wing flaps. And I could walk there. Proctor Electric before the war made toasters and irons.
DUNHAM: So it was closer.
KOHL: I could walk there from my mother's house. And also there I worked 4:00 to 12:00. So at one o'clock in the morning I was walking like five blocks or six blocks to my mother's house by myself.
DUNHAM: And there was never any problem, and that was never a concern for your family?
KOHL: Never looked over my shoulder. And my mother wasn't concerned that I was walking alone at night in the neighborhood, or taking the subway, or taking the streetcar. There wasn't a reason to be concerned. And I did.
DUNHAM: How was Philadelphia changing over the war years or the country from what you were observing? What changes did you see during that time?
KOHL: You mean now?
DUNHAM: Or while you were living in it or looking back. But I think mostly when you were in that moment, what did you notice?
KOHL: Well, I know my mother's neighborhood now. I went back to see my house I guess about ten years ago, maybe.
DUNHAM: We could certainly talk about changes there. I guess what I meant was from the onset of the war, during the war years, how did you see Philadelphia change during that time?
KOHL: Well, I think people became more aware. Oh, I was an air raid warden between all this. I was an air raid warden when I was in high school. See, I have claustrophobia, and I could not see myself sitting in my mother's house with black curtains, closed in. I made an air raid shelter in our basement, like they told us to, with food and canned foods and gallons of water. I cleaned it out. We put furniture. If we had to, it would be there. I did that when I was in high school, because it was '41 when we were bombed at Pearl Harbor, and then they said we had--my mother had to make black curtains. Everybody's windows had to be covered. When I think back, my mother's neighborhood was so far from any kind of a Navy yard or any kind of a facility would be--what would you call--that they would bomb.
DUNHAM: Well, looking back. But everyone was in that same approach.
KOHL: Me and two of the guys in the neighborhood, I guess I was fourteen, fifteen, walked around with helmets on our head and flashlights to make sure everything was in our area, my neighborhood, that had the blackout.
DUNHAM: Had you specifically applied for that, or how did you become--?
KOHL: Oh, yes.
DUNHAM: What was the process to become an air raid warden?
KOHL: I don't even remember who recruited me. I don't. I just know I applied for it, and I got the job. Volunteer, of course. Everything was volunteer. And I did. I still have my first aid kit that I got, they gave me. I have it here.
DUNHAM: Oh, I'd love to see that--
KOHL: I'll show it to you.
DUNHAM: --when we take a break. What was it like? Did you ever have to use it?
KOHL: No. I never did.
DUNHAM: But you were ready.
KOHL: Thank goodness we never had to use anything. Thank goodness nothing ever happened in my neighborhood or anyone's neighborhood in Philadelphia. There wasn't any problems but--
DUNHAM: You mentioned Pearl Harbor. Do you recall where you were when you first heard the news of Pearl Harbor?
KOHL: I guess my father had it on the radio. He had the news on the radio. Lowell Thomas or one of the news--
DUNHAM: It was a Sunday, so--
KOHL: It was a Sunday. I had a friend that was there in Pearl--he had enlisted in the Navy, and he was killed at Pearl Harbor. A good friend. And, in fact, I had two friends there, but one friend didn't get hurt. But one friend was hurt. Was killed. And it was devastating. We were all so young. He enlisted, and that's why when they asked for volunteers I volunteered.
DUNHAM: Had they been older classmates of yours, or just friends you knew otherwise?
KOHL: I guess he was a couple years older than me. Maybe he was eighteen.
DUNHAM: So you're a senior. Did many of the boys then leave before the end of the school year? Your classmates?
KOHL: No, not that I know of. No. It was just a very sad part of our life, the whole thing that happened to us. And now I don't know. It seems like we don't learn a lesson. Perpetuates.
DUNHAM: It's challenging to be sure. The woman we interviewed earlier in the week, she was a little later in the war, but the nursing program she entered was connected with the military. If the war had still been going on than she would have been a military nurse. Was your program like that, or it was a civilian--?
KOHL: I wanted to enlist in the Army. My cousin was in the WAVES, and I wanted to enlist. My mother wouldn't let me. I could be an air raid warden, I could work at the Navy Yard, but I couldn't enlist.
DUNHAM: What would your mother have said?
KOHL: She didn't want me to.
DUNHAM: She just said--
KOHL: Oh, there was no--
DUNHAM: There was no explanation? It was just--
KOHL: No.
DUNHAM: You said your father was a quiet man. Would you have checked in with him, too, or that was it? Kind of--
KOHL: No, that was it. There was no discussion. Yeah.
DUNHAM: Did you ever consider that later on, or once that was said you knew that wasn't a possibility?
KOHL: No.
DUNHAM: Your cousin who was in the WAVES, do you know much about her experience?
KOHL: No, she's from California. My father's family. But she did come to visit us in her uniform, and I was so--she was older than me, of course--was so excited. But it wasn't even an option.
DUNHAM: So what was the transition when you went from the Navy Yard to--remind me where you went.
KOHL: Proctor Electric.
DUNHAM: I know you were still on the swing shift, but what was different, what was the same?
KOHL: It was the same thing. I was riveting. I was a riveter with the wing flaps there. They were doing Navy wing flaps. And when they transitioned back to toasters and irons I worked there for a few months as a civilian, and then I got married.
DUNHAM: So after the end of the war you were able to stay on as they transitioned back to--yeah.
KOHL: Oh, yeah. I could have stayed there.
DUNHAM: So you worked from '42 all the way through '45 between the Navy Yard and Proctor. At the Navy Yard or at Proctor were there many people that came from other parts of the country during the course of the war? Did they expand a lot such that--?
KOHL: Not that I know of. The only expansion was only servicemen from all over the country came into Philadelphia in the Navy Yard. And the Army bases, the soldiers. It was a big naval base, and they were shipping out from there, yes.
DUNHAM: So they had a graveyard and a day shift at both the jobs you did. Did you have any crossover with the day shift or the graveyard, or were there ever any challenges with the transition?
KOHL: No. Everyone got along. I don't remember any kind of a discussion or animosity about anything. I just did my job, and made friends with people, and it was just real nice.
DUNHAM: Although people were working very harmoniously and all, there also were some accidents on the job. And, of course, you're studying nursing. Did you see or hear--weren't aware of that?
KOHL: No, I wasn't involved in anything like that. I just had to be careful for myself. The girl I worked with, we had to be very careful.
DUNHAM: Did you have any close calls?
KOHL: No. Not really. No.
DUNHAM: Was there one main girl that you worked with consistently, or did you have a variety of partners?
KOHL: No, I had one, usually one person, we worked together. Because you have to do it harmoniously. You had to go down the line. So she had to be right--right down the line with her backing up with her--she was there, and you were there, and we just worked together. I don't remember any problems at all.
DUNHAM: Do you remember where your partner was from, what her name was?
KOHL: Yes, the one girl that I worked with at Proctor, on the picture, her name was Grace Kelly, and that was before the Grace Kelly. Nice girl. She was from Kensington, I think, part of the city. And I lost track. I got married, I moved away from the city, and my husband went to school on the GI Bill. He was a serviceman, a flight engineer with the Air Force. And I met him, we got married. He went to--
DUNHAM: How did you meet?
KOHL: Through family. And my--
DUNHAM: During the war?
KOHL: No, when it was over.
DUNHAM: When he came back.
KOHL: When he came back. He came back November of 1945. He was stationed in the Middle East.
DUNHAM: I was going to say, what was his name and where did he serve?
KOHL: My husband was Raymond Kohl, K-O-H-L. And he was stationed in the Middle East. He used to fly. He was with the Air Transport Command, and he flew spies from Cairo, Egypt. In fact, when I moved here, his uniform I donated to the Philadelphia Museum of Art for their collection, with all his ribbons and all his stripes and everything. So they have his uniform there. I think it's on display. It was on display when they have the World War II exhibit. And I had given his uniform there because it was in such good shape, and he was so proud. My husband was so proud. He had enlisted when he was seventeen, right out of high school. So then when he came back in '45 is when he went on the GI Bill to school. And yes. His uniform and his coat, that heavy big winter coat is at the Philadelphia Museum of Art.
DUNHAM: That's very nice. Did he grow up in Philadelphia?
KOHL: Yes. But I didn't know him when he was a little boy. My family knew him. It was funny. After I met him through the family, my mother remembered that she knew his mother.
DUNHAM: So what was it like when you first were dating and met him? It was instant? Instant? Okay. He was a good dancer?
KOHL: He was a fabulous dancer. That did it for both of us. He was.
DUNHAM: He could do the jitterbug even?
KOHL: When we started to dance everybody would form a circle. It was great.
DUNHAM: I'll bet.
KOHL: Yeah. I was married forty-nine years. Didn't make my fiftieth.
DUNHAM: I'm sorry.
KOHL: I have a girlfriend's married sixty-two years. Talked to her the other day.
DUNHAM: What do you remember about the end of the war? Hearing of V-E Day, V-J Day? Do you remember where you were when--?
KOHL: No, I don't, but I know it was exciting. Very exciting.
DUNHAM: Were there big events?
KOHL: Parades, yes.
DUNHAM: Parades in Philadelphia.
KOHL: Parades.
DUNHAM: All the soldiers, some of the soldiers returning.
KOHL: I told you, Philadelphia went all out for the soldiers, the sailors, or servicemen, I should say, because from all three branches. In fact, the Coast Guards. My cousin was a Coast Guard. Yeah. My brother was in the service. He was stationed in Paris, my brother, in Europe. Everybody I knew. All my family was in one branch or another of the service.
DUNHAM: Was your brother drafted?
KOHL: No, he enlisted. My brother enlisted, too, right out of high school.
DUNHAM: I was wondering, was there a significant Jewish community where you grew up?
KOHL: Not where I lived. No. Not in my neighborhood. But it didn't matter. We were all born and raised there. It was like everybody knew everyone and respected everyone. Yes.
DUNHAM: What was the ethnic makeup of the rest of your neighborhood?
KOHL: Oh, my gosh. I can't remember now. I could call Marguerite. She would tell me. Marguerite grew up across the street from me. St. Henry Parish. It didn't seem to make any difference. In fact, Marguerite, I still talk to her. She's still living. She lives in Pennsylvania. We grew up together.
DUNHAM: Synagogue was a little ways away from your neighborhood?
KOHL: Yes. Took the bus, took the subway. Walked, whatever.
DUNHAM: Then when did you first hear about the atrocities in Europe?
KOHL: Never. We didn't know what was going on there. My parents didn't know. I know they didn't know. I mean, people started bringing people over, but nobody ever talked about it. Nobody said anything. We just didn't know how bad it was or what was going on really. But when I think about it, we didn't have television. We had radio. I don't remember the news saying much about what was going on over there except we were at war.
DUNHAM: Did you go to the movies and see the newsreels and that type of thing?
KOHL: Yes.
DUNHAM: So you got some kind of news there of certain aspect--
KOHL: Yes, but it wasn't--
DUNHAM: It wasn't that.
KOHL: Not what we learned later. No. The atrocities, never knew about it.
DUNHAM: Did you know any German or Italian Americans growing up or during the war in your community?
KOHL: Yes. I guess it was a mixture of families where I lived. Marguerite, she was Catholic. Helen Beck next door to me was Protestant. We just all grew up together. Religion didn't matter to anybody, what we were.
DUNHAM: Well, the reason I ask is on the West Coast, you may know, where there were a lot of Japanese Americans who were, of course, incarcerated.
KOHL: I heard about that, and that was a disgrace.
DUNHAM: Yeah. In the East Coast it didn't happen as much. In a very few cases they did incarcerate German and Italian Americans, and then there were also some prisoners of war sometimes that people encountered. Because the East Coast was more sensitive to Germany and Italy, sometimes there were more issues of people being uncomfortable or prejudiced to even citizens, Germans and Italian Americans. So I just wondered if that ever--
KOHL: I wasn't aware of it. My family was not aware of it. I can honestly say that. Not in my neighborhood where I grew up. But it could have been. I don't know.
DUNHAM: How long was your nursing school program?
KOHL: It was two years.
DUNHAM: What happened when you finished it? Did you pursue nursing?
KOHL: No, my husband didn't like it. My mother didn't like it.
DUNHAM: Why was that?
KOHL: They didn't like it. My mother didn't want me to be a nurse. Thinking back, I was thinking about that the other day. I think the hospitals--and I may be wrong, I'd have to check it, I never did check--but I think it was the smell. I think in those days, if I'm remembering, ether was used, and I think clung to the clothes. Well, my mother just didn't like it.
DUNHAM: Had there been any discussion before you had gone into the nursing program?
KOHL: Yeah, but that I was determined with. That was my thing. And even though she didn't like it I determined I was going to do it.
DUNHAM: Did you debate with her when you finished?
KOHL: No. You didn't debate with your mother or father.
DUNHAM: You did enough to go to school.
KOHL: I did what I did, but I went to work, and I found the time to do it, so it didn't distract me from doing the job.
DUNHAM: And so was there a cost for your nursing school, and were you paying it?
KOHL: Oh, yeah. Everything.
DUNHAM: With your wages.
KOHL: What I'm saying is, I could do both, and that's how it worked out.
DUNHAM: Did you ever have any regrets about not pursuing nursing?
KOHL: No. Because when they needed me, I was there for my mother, my father, my husband.
DUNHAM: The skills you had learned in nursing?
KOHL: Yes.
DUNHAM: Well, that's wonderful.
KOHL: And my daughter. I was there for her. Ielyne died of breast cancer 49 years.
DUNHAM: We talked a little about your mother and father's background, but when we talked on the phone you mentioned he was a veteran of both World War I.
KOHL: Jewish war veterans-- member.
DUNHAM: So what was his service experience? Do you know?
KOHL: I think he drove an ambulance. My father was stationed in France. I have pictures of him in his uniform.
DUNHAM: Okay, we could look. Yeah, that would be great to see.
KOHL: Have to think where I put them.
DUNHAM: We're going to take a break in a few minutes so we could--
KOHL: I have to find out where they are. I don't know where they are.
DUNHAM: No worries.
KOHL: Yes. In fact, I can show you, I have a citation from the king of England to my father. Well, to the servicemen. But I have it framed right up there.
DUNHAM: Did you participate, or your family, in victory gardens, or do you remember how rationing impacted you? Can you talk a little bit about those experiences?
KOHL: Yes, rationing. We had rationing, and my mother bought me silk stockings on the black market.
DUNHAM: Oh, you did get silk stockings.
KOHL: Yes, she did. I don't know where she got them, but she got me silk stockings.
DUNHAM: Had you done the paint-on thing until you got--?
KOHL: I did the paint-on, yes. Oh, yes. Did both because silk stockings tear. But I did. I had the leg coloring for my legs.
DUNHAM: Were there certain foods that you didn't really have access to then during the war years because of the rationing? Or were there other things that your mom found a way to get that were hard to get? Like the silk stockings?
KOHL: No. We were big vegetable--not much meat. Some chicken. I don't remember ever going hungry. Meat and potatoes or potatoes and vegetables.
DUNHAM: I know you had food when you got off work. What would you take for lunch, or what would you have for lunch or dinner when you were at school? Would you bring a lunch and dinner? Do you recall?
KOHL: Bread and butter sandwich or maybe a cheese sandwich or baloney sandwich. Yeah. I can't explain it but it wasn't like--I can't remember not having something to eat, even if it was just bread and butter. We always had bread and butter.
DUNHAM: When you were at work, I know you worked the swing shift, but did they have events there or anything during breaks? Was there a sense of patriotism, especially at the job or elsewhere?
KOHL: Yeah. Everybody knew what we were doing and what we were doing it for. Like I say, everybody worked together. I don't remember any problems with people fighting or anything like that. We were there to do a job, and it was for the country.
DUNHAM: Do you remember your boss?
KOHL: No. I don't remember. I don't even remember the one at Proctor even.
DUNHAM: What did you wear?
KOHL: I wore coveralls. We had to wear coveralls and put your hair up. Yeah. A net. Like a snood, they called them, for your hair.
DUNHAM: Yeah. And were you comfortable in your work clothes?
KOHL: I guess I didn't know any better. It was what it was. You never question. It was what you were supposed to do.
DUNHAM: I ask because I know you're very fashionable to this day, and so I just wondered. It's this question of--
KOHL: When you're a teenager you're as fashionable as you can be, especially when there's no money around. You just do the best you can with bobby socks and whatever. Yeah, my mother made my clothes. She sewed. If I saw something she would make it.
DUNHAM: Where would you find something? Like in a catalog?
KOHL: Well, let me tell you. No, not catalog. Well, Sears Catalog. That was our form of entertainment. The girl next door and me would sit on a rainy day and go through the Sears Catalog and pick out the clothes we thought we'd like to have if we could afford them. But when I had to have my prom dress my mother took me down to Center City Philadelphia, the most expensive department store. And, of course, in those days they didn't make petites. I'm small. But she would say to me, "Pick out the gown that you like the best," that I like and whatever color I would like. Whatever I liked. But the style was in this expensive department store. Not the basement but on the fourth floor or third floor where they had women's dresses. And I would try on what I liked. Of course, even small was too big. And she would look at it and, "That's the style you like?" I'd say, "Yes, what do you think? You like it? You like the color?" And she'd look at it, and she'd study it and turn me around in it. "Okay." She'd go home, she'd buy the fabric, take a newspaper and lay it on the floor, and cut it out, the pattern, try it on me, and make my dress.
DUNHAM: And then you had a prom dress.
KOHL: Just like the dress I saw in the department store.
DUNHAM: How did she learn to do that?
KOHL: She was a seamstress, my mother. My mother and my aunt.
DUNHAM: For hire?
KOHL: Yes. They worked in a factory. I have to tell you this story. My mother's an identical twin. She and Aunt Ruth were identical. You couldn't tell them apart. Of course, we could, but no one else could tell them apart. Even though they didn't dress alike they had the same taste. Of course my Aunt Ruth would go to Philadelphia to the department store, and she'd call my mother and say, "I bought a really nice dress on sale I think you would like. They're having a sale on ladies' dresses. Go down and see. It's a great buy." So my mother would go the next day or two days later, and Aunt Ruth would tell her where the sale was in the store. My mother would go down and buy a dress. She didn't see what Aunt Ruth bought. It'd be the same dress, a different color.
DUNHAM: Wow.
KOHL: That's how much alike they were. Or my mother would be on the Frankford El or the subway, and someone would say, "Hello, Mrs. Kaplan." Or my aunt. "Hello." It was my aunt's neighbor, would think it was my aunt. But they always said hello because they knew that it was one or the other's neighbor, depending on where they were at the time.
Fukumoto:That's funny.
KOHL: Nobody could tell the difference.
DUNHAM: Did they ever play any tricks on anyone with that?
KOHL: Yes. My mother told me this story. When she was working in a factory, I told you, seamstress, my aunt and she worked together. And my mother set the needle. My aunt, her hand slipped, the needle went through her finger, so of course she had to go to the doctor and had to have it bandaged up. She couldn't go to the work. So the boss says, "If you can't work you don't have a job." So my mother went and did the job for her. He didn't know the difference. Just until her finger got better so she went back to work.
DUNHAM: Did they start that work as young girls?
KOHL: Yes.
DUNHAM: How old were they, do you know, when they started?
KOHL: No, I don't. Maybe sixteen, seventeen. I don't know.
DUNHAM: And do you know how long they did it?
KOHL: Until they got married. I don't know. But they didn't work after they got married, either one of them, my mother or Aunt Ruth.
DUNHAM: They both had that skill.
KOHL: Oh, yes. My cousins were dressed the same way.
DUNHAM: And did you learn?
KOHL: Not that skill. I sew and I hem and I repair, but I could never do that.
DUNHAM: I kind of wanted to ask what a typical day was like for your mom. Obviously she was very resourceful and was raising you. But do you know what kind of a typical day was like for her when you were growing up?
KOHL: Yeah. Clean the house, cook, sweep the porch. I had to sweep the porch. If I wanted to go to the movies I had to sweep the porch to get the ten cents to go to the movies. But I guess just a housewife. I don't know.
DUNHAM: Like having that type of seamstress ability is exceptional.
KOHL: Oh, you mean do outside work for people? No.
DUNHAM: No, no. I just meant--
KOHL: No.
DUNHAM: Or you said you didn't learn that from her. But what other things did you learn from your mom? I'll ask that.
KOHL: Well, I learned how to sew and crochet, clean, polish, sweep the porch and the steps and the sidewalk. We did that. And I guess be nice to people.
DUNHAM: Well, what about from your grandparents? You had these Sunday suppers.
KOHL: They were wonderful.
DUNHAM: How long did they go on for? Was that into your--?
KOHL: My grandfather died on my sixteenth birthday. That was very hard for me. And my grandmother, oh, I guess it was maybe ten years later that she died.
DUNHAM: Was she able to continue to have the suppers?
KOHL: No, no. She downsized. She had a big home in Philadelphia. My mother and my aunt got her a nice apartment, took care of her. We all took care of her.
DUNHAM: Was the family able to still come together pretty often or special events then more?
KOHL: Well, not like that but we all got together. We still get together. They all came to my party.
DUNHAM: Right, right. No, that's what I--
KOHL: The ones that are still here all came to my party. They came from California to my party, from North Carolina.
DUNHAM: Well, that's what I was getting at, I think, is that strong sense of family that clearly you grew up with and that you still embody to this day. You said the annual family trips that you take, and so I just was thinking of that. And that is something that not everyone has, even then and certainly not now. So I was just thinking of that.
KOHL: Oh, my goodness.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Well, let's take a little break and then maybe we could do a little bit more if that's okay.
KOHL: Am I being helpful?
DUNHAM: Yeah, absolutely.
KOHL: [looking at photographs] See, that's me and my husband. He was so hand--that's my daughter. That's Ielyne. She died of breast cancer.
DUNHAM: Oh, I'm sorry. How old was she when she passed? 49 years old.
KOHL: Oh, and that's my Ethan with the red convertible; the top goes up. So you know what I said to him? Because I always wanted a red convertible. But down here, I couldn't. I have a car, but the salt air kills everything. I could not have a convertible down in Atlantic City. The salt air eats everything up. So I said, "I'm going to buy you a red convertible." That's my granddaughter and her husband. That's my husband.
DUNHAM: It is still July 2, 2014, and this is tape two with Regina Kohl. Before I ask anything, is there anything else about the war years that maybe I haven't asked you about or that you've thought of that you would like to share, especially about that period? I welcome your thoughts, if so.
KOHL: I just felt there was a lot of camaraderie and a lot of people cared. I'm sure everyone cares today; they just don't understand the continuous involvement of the United States where we're not wanted. That's all.
02-00:00:53
DUNHAM: I was wondering, when you were working at the naval yard and at Proctor Electric, did you see--or just not necessarily at the workplaces but elsewhere, at the theaters--images of not necessarily Rosie the Riveter but women working in the workplace and that type of iconography?
KOHL: Oh, yes.
DUNHAM: So what do you remember about those images at the time, or how you felt working in those roles?
KOHL: I think that's when women wearing slacks was born, because before that I don't remember--my mother never wore slacks. In fact, she never wore slacks. Everything was dresses or skirts, even school, when I went to school, the girls didn't wear slacks. Bobby socks. I think that was the beginning of women in slacks and suits. Because I know we had to wear coveralls, and that was a new thing.
DUNHAM: So did you begin to wear slacks during that time period outside of work?
KOHL: No, I don't remember. Not really.
DUNHAM: But you may have?
KOHL: And jeans. Of course, there was no jeans or anything like that. No. We still wore dresses. When I went dancing I wore dresses or skirts.
DUNHAM: But it was during that period that you began to see--
KOHL: --swing around--
DUNHAM: --some women, and maybe in the subsequent years, more dramatically--
KOHL: Oh, yes. Yes. But I remember, even after I was married, '47, '48, '49, I think women still wore skirts more than they wore slacks. Then it evolved into slacks and jeans and things.
DUNHAM: I know it wasn't as open at the time, but did you know of any gays or lesbians during that time period?
KOHL: No. But I had an experience. Want me to tell you about it?
DUNHAM: Please.
KOHL: A friend of mine, a neighbor my age, married a man thirty-five years old, and he owned a club in Philadelphia. And she was bored. She didn't work. So she called me and asked me if I would come down and go to the movies with her in Center City, Philadelphia. She was a neighbor friend, and I said I would. So she said, "Well, meet me at the club." So I went to the club, and of course her husband knew that I wasn't of age for drink--no drinking. So he brought me a ginger ale, and I'm sitting in a booth all dressed up waiting for her to go to the movies. She hadn't arrived to meet me. I'm sitting in the club. At the bar are all these young people, women and men, young women and young men, and a couple of the women were waving at me and smiling, and I'm waving and smiling back. So when she came I said to her, "Gee, this is a friendly place." She said, "What do you mean?" I said, "Well, those girls at the bar and different things are waving." She says, "Let's get out of here." She said, "They're all lesbians." I didn't know what a lesbian was. But when she said that--don't print it.
DUNHAM: Well, you'll have a chance to review it. But the option is to seal the material until a later date, too. Because that is the type of information that isn't talked about much, and it's important to know that all people existed even then, right?
KOHL: Yes. And I didn't know anything about anyone or anything about that at all at sixteen years old, seventeen. I didn't know anything about that. Wouldn't be any reason for me to know.
DUNHAM: So you think the club, though, in any event, was a place where the gay and/or lesbians regularly visited?
KOHL: That's what she said. It was a gay bar. Her husband owned it. But I didn't know that. She didn't tell me, and I didn't even think about it at the time. It wasn't even a thought. Or anything.
DUNHAM: Again, you could seal it or whatever. But do you know what the bar was called? Do you recall?
KOHL: I don't remember anymore.
DUNHAM: But where was it about? I know it was a long time ago.
KOHL: I was only there once because then we met at the movies. I don't remember. I really don't. But it was in Center City, Philadelphia. He was a very nice man. Her husband was a very nice man and very polite. That was his business. None of my business. Of course, I'm sure he had other people come in, the sailors and soldiers. It was an open bar and club. I was sitting in a booth; it was a club. But my first and only experience with anything connected like that.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Well, since we're talking about bars and clubs, maybe--and this could be after the war, too--[phone ringing] Oh, should we pause for a sec? You want to--
DUNHAM: We're back. I was curious. Talking about clubs. I know you talked about the dances during the war. But I was wondering, and this could be even after the war, about the Philadelphia nightlife, and clubs, and places you went to. Are there any you particularly remember or where you went dancing after you met your husband, too? Can you tell us about that?
KOHL: Yeah. We went to the Y dancing. We went to a couple of clubs dancing.
DUNHAM: The Jewish Y or the YMCA?
KOHL: Both. Wherever there was a dance we went.
DUNHAM: Does the Jewish Y still exist there?
KOHL: Yeah, it's at Broad and Pine in Philadelphia.
DUNHAM: So what was it like? Did it have live music?
KOHL: Live music and plenty of food; everybody could eat. Even the hostesses could have something to eat. It was for the servicemen, but whatever was there you could help yourself to also. Everybody.
DUNHAM: And what was your job as a hostess? How did that work?
KOHL: We all dressed up and go dancing. And you'd go to the Y, or you'd go to the Reyburn Plaza, or to the USO, or the Stage Door Canteen, and they had hostesses there. Women older. Would be my age. Well, not my age, but your age there now, that age. For me, for sixteen year old, seventeen year old. And it was just dancing. There was nothing else.
DUNHAM: Was it just open dancing, or did you have to have like a ticket?
KOHL: No. Well, you had to have identification. I have cards, identification cards. Oh, I have it.
DUNHAM: For the different clubs you had a card?
KOHL: Yeah, I had a Stage Door Canteen with my picture.
DUNHAM: Oh, you still have those? If they're handy.
KOHL: I have to think about that.
DUNHAM: Let's make a note to check at the end and maybe we'll take a shot of those. I would love to see them.
KOHL: We had to have identification when we went in, sure. Not anybody could just go in. When I mean anyone, I mean we had identification. I had identification to go in and dance with the soldiers.
DUNHAM: Did you have to apply for the position then?
KOHL: Yes.
DUNHAM: I'm just curious--again, sorry for not knowing the etiquette--I've heard about this a lot, but I don't know if I've asked or clarified. You could dance with the same soldier kind of all-night?
KOHL: It didn't matter. It didn't matter. I don't remember it making any difference. You could dance.
DUNHAM: After the war did sort of the club dancing change much, in Philadelphia particularly?
KOHL: No, I still think they had the canteens open for a while and things like that. But then everybody went home. The boys all went home. There wasn't any need for anything like that. It wasn't for me anymore, but I think they had--I don't know if they still do, but I think they had places open where a soldier or sailor could get a meal. Or a serviceman, I should say, could get a meal if they wanted to. I just heard a story yesterday where this friend of mine was in a restaurant, a takeout restaurant, and a soldier walked in, and her friend paid for his meal, the serviceman. Didn't know him but said, "I'll pay for you, and thank you for helping us." That I heard yesterday, and this was ongoing at the Second World War.
DUNHAM: That was the norm then?
KOHL: Yes, that was the norm.
DUNHAM: The restaurant owners would do it?
KOHL: The restaurant owners, or if you saw someone, a serviceman, that wanted to have something to eat. They didn't have to pay most of the times.
DUNHAM: So how soon after the war did you get married?
KOHL: I got married in '46.
DUNHAM: And what was the transition to married life like?
KOHL: Student. My husband was a student.
DUNHAM: So were you working while he was a student?
KOHL: I was working.
DUNHAM: What were you doing at that time?
KOHL: Well, I became a bookkeeper. Didn't know anything about bookkeeping but I became a bookkeeper. I learned.
DUNHAM: How was that?
KOHL: It was great. Everything I did was great.
DUNHAM: I can tell. [laughs] I think it must be you.
KOHL: It was like an adventure. It's like I have an adventure when I go to New York every month now. I'm not going July and August, because they're coming here, and it's hot. I'm at the country club. But after they start school in September, I have to go meet the teacher.
DUNHAM: You meet your great-grandchildren's teachers?
KOHL: Absolutely.
DUNHAM: Wow, that's great. What is that conversation like? What if they don't pass muster? [laughter]
KOHL: When they start school--in fact, it was funny. This was the first year I hadn't met Ethan's teacher. But she said to me, my granddaughter, "With all the security in schools today they don't want people walking in." A couple of times they had parent-teacher night I wasn't in New York to go meet the teacher. So--I was there two weeks ago; I'm home a week--she said to me, "You want to walk over, pick up the kids from school?" I said, "Sure." She doesn't live far. We walk. It's a small town. Walked over. We're sitting on the bench waiting for the kids to come out of school. It was about ten minutes of 3:00, and she and I are sitting together, my granddaughter and I, waiting for them to come out. The door opens in the school, and this young women comes out, and Orange says to me, "Oh, Grandmom, that's Ethan's teacher." She comes walking straight over, and she says, "I had to meet Ethan's grandmother." My granddaughter introduced me, and she said, "Well, I had to come out and meet Ethan's teacher." She gave me a kiss. And she said, "I have to go back in school and dismiss the children." That's the first time I met that teacher, because all the other teachers I know now. The teacher that Indigo's going to have next year for second grade, I met her because Ethan had her. You understand?
DUNHAM: Yeah, but let me understand this. You're saying last fall you didn't meet Ethan's teacher because you were a security risk? [laughter]
KOHL: No. No, no. Because the school has such strict security.
DUNHAM: That great-grandparents aren't allowed on campus.
KOHL: Well, I don't know. She didn't make a point about it.
DUNHAM: Sorry, I'm just--
KOHL: And I didn't make an issue, and you're teasing me.
DUNHAM: No, I just wanted to make sure I heard that right.
KOHL: No, no. And it was just that--
DUNHAM: I'm not teasing. I'm just wondering about the state of judgment.
KOHL: The schools are--no.
DUNHAM: This is in Long Island.
KOHL: She didn't ask, and we didn't get into it.
DUNHAM: Sure. No, I understand.
KOHL: It didn't work. But when she came out--
DUNHAM: Yeah. But I'm glad you got a chance to meet her. That was very nice.
KOHL: Meet the teacher.
DUNHAM: And I think it's remarkable that you are connected. I know we've talked about you sharing books with your great-grandson, and it's great. Well, let's go back a little bit farther again if you don't mind. So you were a bookkeeper for a number of years, then, while your husband was finishing school? Or did you do some other things then, or how did that unfold?
KOHL: What did I do?
DUNHAM: You guys got your own place in Philadelphia, or staying with family?
KOHL: No, I was living in West Virginia. He went to school in Buckhannon, West Virginia.
DUNHAM: What school was that?
KOHL: West Virginia Wesleyan College. When the fellows came back from service, all the Philadelphia colleges, Penn State, Temple, Drexel--I'm trying to think--LaSalle, all the colleges had to take back all the students that had been drafted.
DUNHAM: Who were already enrolled.
KOHL: Already enrolled. My husband had graduated high school and went right in the Air Force, so he wasn't connected to a college. So he went down to Temple, and the counselor told him there was a special engineering course opening in Buckhannon, West Virginia. So he went there. Then we got married, and I went to live in Buckhannon, West Virginia.
DUNHAM: So what was West Virginia like for a Philly girl?
KOHL: Very small town. No buses, no streetcars. I think the lights went out at nine o'clock. It was great fun.
DUNHAM: That's you again. So what was fun about it?
KOHL: It was a whole different lifestyle than I had ever been with. I didn't know anything.
DUNHAM: You were working day shift now as a bookkeeper at first at least?
KOHL: No, no. In Buckhannon, West Virginia, I worked at the student union, and then I worked as a short order cook. Five o'clock in the morning I'm making coffee and scrambled eggs in this little tiny luncheonette so we could get breakfast. Great. Everything I did was good.
DUNHAM: The glass is more than half-full. How long were you in West Virginia?
KOHL: We were there a year, and then we went to Dayton, Ohio.
DUNHAM: He transferred?
KOHL: The engineering course was one year in Buckhannon, West Virginia. Well, he continued at University of Dayton, engineering. Dayton, Ohio.
DUNHAM: Is it halfway between the small town in West Virginia and Philly, or what was it like? Or is it closer to the West Virginia experience?
KOHL: No, it was a little more.
DUNHAM: How was that then? What did you do there?
KOHL: That's when I became a bookkeeper.
DUNHAM: So you picked that up. And how many years are you in--?
KOHL: One year. Then the engineering course continued in Dayton, Ohio. My husband worked at Wright Patterson Air Force Base. My girls were born in Dayton. I was there three years. Then we came to Philadelphia.
DUNHAM: This is then late forties that they're born, or into fifties?
KOHL: They were fifties. And then we came back to Philadelphia.
DUNHAM: After he finished his engineering?
KOHL: Graduated and got his degree, University of Dayton.
DUNHAM: Did you live in the city, or did you live in the suburbs when you came back?
KOHL: No, I didn't live in the city of Philadelphia. I lived in Philadelphia but never in Center City.
DUNHAM: Where did you live? And I apologize for not knowing the area that well.
KOHL: I guess you'd call it the suburbs.
DUNHAM: Was it a district? Did it have a particular name?
KOHL: The Great. Northeast Philadelphia. We had a house.
DUNHAM: Had it changed much since you'd been gone those four or five years?
KOHL: Oh, not then, no. Things were the same. My mother and father were in their home. It was the same. But over the years.
DUNHAM: But your daughters grew up in Philadelphia?
KOHL: Yes.
DUNHAM: Then your husband got a job in engineering, or teaching?
KOHL: Oh, engineering. Then he went back and got his master's in education at Temple, so then he had his own consulting business, engineering consulting business, and he was teaching at Holy Family College in northeast Philadelphia. And then the High School of Performing Arts.
DUNHAM: What did he teach there?
KOHL: Chemistry and physics.
DUNHAM: And when did you become a teacher?
KOHL: After my girls went back to school. After my girls grew up.
DUNHAM: Finished school.
KOHL: I went to work for the Philadelphia school district in a brand new school where I lived.
DUNHAM: How was that?
KOHL: Twenty-five years.
DUNHAM: Oh, you taught twenty-five years there?
KOHL: Twenty-five, it was the science department.
DUNHAM: Where did you go to school for that?
KOHL: Temple. Penn State.
DUNHAM: Yeah. In brief, but what was your teaching career like there?
KOHL: Twenty-five years. I loved it.
DUNHAM: At the same school? All in Philly anyway?
KOHL: Oh, yes, Philadelphia.
DUNHAM: High school?
KOHL: Middle school.
DUNHAM: Different grade levels?
KOHL: Sixth, seventh, and eighth grade. I loved it. I loved the kids. It was wonderful. Retired there with twenty-five years.
DUNHAM: So what year about did you retire then?
KOHL: I think it was '93.
DUNHAM: You retired but not really. You retired from teaching. You continue living life to the fullest, right?
KOHL: Well, I teach art. Now, I'm a volunteer at Our Lady Star of the Sea, Catholic parochial school here in Atlantic City. And I volunteer one day a week. I've got the cutest kids, fourth grade. Oh, they're so adorable.
DUNHAM: Did your daughter initially learn art through you then? Did you have a passion for art that you shared?
KOHL: I guess. She was wonderful.
DUNHAM: What kind of art did you particularly like or do you particularly like doing?
KOHL: I like everything. Colors.
DUNHAM: Well, I was wondering, just looking back, especially on the war years and your experience working your two different jobs--
KOHL: Three different jobs, four different jobs.
DUNHAM: Three different jobs, excuse me. Many, many jobs over the years.
KOHL: Five jobs. Many jobs.
DUNHAM: But particularly looking back at that period of time, is there anything you take away from it? The transition and opportunities for women that occurred then? What's your perspective, I guess, in your own life and for others looking back?
KOHL: I think it was good. I think it was very good. I think it let people know that women could do just as good, if not better, then men in many things. And following through.
DUNHAM: And how do you think it impacted your own life?
KOHL: I don't know. It's just that I don't mind doing something new or learning something new or sharing with other people what I know or don't know.
DUNHAM: I know you've had a lot of other experiences and done a lot of volunteering. What do you do? You just celebrated your ninetieth birthday earlier this year. Congratulations. Had a great big party. But what all are you up to these days? You just mentioned you volunteer and teach art. What else do you do to keep yourself thriving?
KOHL: The Jewish Community Center here in Margate, which is ten minutes from here, like any city you have sections. You're from California, so there is LA, and then you go a little further it's Hollywood, then it's Ventura. You only have to go three or four blocks and you're in another--it's the same here. If you go three blocks from here you're in Ventnor. Then you go six more blocks, you're in Margate. And then you go a little further and you're in Longport. It's different, what do you call them, counties or communities.
DUNHAM: Neighborhoods, districts.
KOHL: Communities. So I have the book club at the Ventnor Library. I still drive.
DUNHAM: You retired from the beauty pageant position?
KOHL: Well, I don't know. The Miss America pageant was here for a hundred years or something. Then they took it away, and they put it in Las Vegas, which was a bust. Now they brought it back last year. So I don't know. I'm going to see if they want me to help them, volunteer with them again. I don't know.
DUNHAM: We talked about it off camera, but maybe you could tell us briefly your role, that you served many years and how many champions or Miss Americas.
KOHL: I had five Miss Americas. We were a committee of fourteen ladies and their husbands. So we were, I won't say in charge, but we entertained the parents of the contestants. Not the contestants. The parents. So we arranged the committee, and it was wonderful. All the activities for the parents when they came to Atlantic City. Well, of course, there's fifty states, fifty-one states. You have parents from Iowa, Kansas, never saw the ocean. Never been out of their state, and now their daughter's a contestant. So they come to a place they know nothing about, and we made them feel at home, the committee. We were in touch with all the parents. So when I say I had five winners, I had their parents, not them. Because there were fourteen of us, so we broke them up. And we had I'll say five parents each and their families. The grandparents would come in for the finals. And we made sure that those parents became acquainted with Atlantic City and we had activities for them. We had parties for them.
DUNHAM: What drew you to working in the Miss America pageant, excuse me, and this opportunity?
KOHL: It was a rare opportunity for me to get into it. It was just something I wanted to do, and it happened. It was amazing that it happened. But I had to be proposed, I had to be interviewed.
DUNHAM: It was very competitive. Well, it seems like one of many good positions for you. Since I brought that up, why don't I also ask you about your participation in the senior pageant?
KOHL: Well, that was very little. That was very little.
DUNHAM: But you did go to the national event for that?
KOHL: Yes.
DUNHAM: Did you enjoy it?
KOHL: I didn't really qualify. I was there, but I--
DUNHAM: So it wasn't as fun as what you do back here?
KOHL: It was different. It was fun. But those other ladies were more qualified than me to be there. At least I felt that way.
DUNHAM: Sometimes beauty pageants or contestants kind of get made fun of in popular culture. I was just wondering how you felt about that.
KOHL: It wasn't a beauty contest.
DUNHAM: Sorry. Miss America. Pardon me.
KOHL: It had to do with--what do you call it--talent. I wasn't talented enough.
DUNHAM: Oh, in the senior competition. But I was wondering about Miss America, which I misnamed a beauty--see how easy it is to happen.
KOHL: Not a beauty contest. I have the new book. Friend of mine gave it to me. It's a scholarship program. It's not a beauty, it's a scholarship program.
DUNHAM: My question is, how do you or others connected with it feel about that it gets reduced to that a lot of times by those not involved in it?
KOHL: Doesn't matter. They are beautiful. They are beautiful. There's no question about they're beautiful, but they are talented and smart. All scholarship, all college. That's part of the requirements. They have to be in college or graduating or something. Besides being talented, to sing or dance or music or whatever. It's a combination. Didn't you ever look at what the Miss America pageant was about?
DUNHAM: I have some sense of it, too, but I have also some sense of, well--
KOHL: You ought to go on the internet and check it out. I'll tell you, these women are unbelievable. They are absolutely incredible women, every one of them. When we used to go, the group--not only do you work with the parents and do all this partying, it's all partying, everything is very social, you're there for the finals. They give you a seat number. You're a part of the whole pageant. And when you watch these young ladies, because we really don't get to meet the young ladies. We meet them, but we don't. We're not part of their day. We're with the parent. But when you see these young ladies on stage--I know I'm glad I'm not a judge because they're so incredible, all of them, and their qualifications.
DUNHAM: Well, we should wrap up. I guess I'll just ask is there anything from any period that I didn't ask about today that you would like to share before we close?
KOHL: Well, I can say I had a wonderful marriage.
DUNHAM: Forty-nine years.
KOHL: Forty-nine years. I wish it had been longer. I had a wonderful marriage. I had two wonderful daughters. I have two wonderful granddaughters and two smart beautiful wonderful great-grandchildren. And we're all tied in together.
DUNHAM: That's wonderful, and it's been an honor to meet you and record some of your story here today.
KOHL: I hope I didn't bore you with all this stuff.
DUNHAM: No, not at--
KOHL: Some of it you have to cut out.
DUNHAM: Yeah, well, we'll have a chance to review it. But I think we'll close today there with that.
KOHL: Well, thank you for coming. I hope there's more. I enjoyed--
DUNHAM: [break in audio] So this was after--
KOHL: My father.
Fukumoto:Nineteen-eighteen. Can you read it?
DUNHAM: Oh. Oh, actually, if I zoom in, I can. From Windsor Castle. King George.
KOHL: I am a volunteer in Israel to the army. I'm a volunteer in the Israeli army. Five times.
DUNHAM: So what do you do as a volunteer?
KOHL: I don't know if I'm going in September or not. Depends on the weather. I volunteer with this group, the Sar-El, which is a volunteer group, people from all over the world, Jews and non-Jews. We had a minister and his wife in my group last time. Two years ago I volunteered. Since 1968 I've been going to Israel. But I've been volunteering since 1972. Well, last time I was there, 2011, I packed medical supplies for the soldiers. Packed and unpacking. No, you have to look at the dates. Take out the old ones, put in new ones. Medical supplies for the field units in Israel. One year we changed the amplifiers in the helmets where they get--when they're on the field and the general or the captain tells them what to do they have the helmets on, and they have these amplifiers built in the helmets, in their hats, and we had to change them and put new ones in. So you sit at a table with all these little tiny components and you work. And you get up at 7:00 in the morning or 6:30, go to breakfast by 7:30, eight o'clock you go to work. It's all volunteer. You work until about 11:30, then you go to lunch. But you live on the base, you sleep in a barracks. You wear the uniform, eat with the soldiers. You live an army life. And I've done that five times, six times.
DUNHAM: For how long of a period of time?
KOHL: Three weeks.
DUNHAM: Is there anything else before we put the camera back away?
KOHL: No. It's a wonderful, wonderful program and it's for everyone. The only reason they take you, or I should say you have to pass a physical. You have to go to your doctor and he has to sign off that you're healthy.
DUNHAM: So when was the last time you were able to go?
KOHL: This in '14? Two thousand eleven. I may go in September. I'm not sure yet. I have a wedding in Israel. So if I go to the wedding, I will volunteer again.
Fukumoto:That's wonderful.
KOHL: You would love it. You two should volunteer. I mean it.
DUNHAM: Yeah, yeah. We've talked about volunteering a lot and how much you've done. It comes from many places. Obviously it comes from family. But I do think also that the World War II, you talked about that spirit of volunteering and the harmonious working, so it seems like a lot in this country of the coming together and transformation was there, as well.
KOHL: This program is so exciting and they treat you so well and the soldiers, they're so young and they say to you, "Thank you for coming." So much respect. There were twenty-six people. This is the first time we had so many people in one unit. Usually they disperse you to different bases. But it was an emergency so we were all together working in this. That's why the group was so big. And like I say, we had a minister there. Belgian. Every county almost volunteer to Israel. Non-Jews. They believe in Israel. They believe in what's happening, and it's a wonderful program.
DUNHAM: Well, thank you for sharing that. I appreciate it.
KOHL: Almost forgot. That's why I said if I change the color of my hair I'll lose my identity. I like being a red head.
